  • Replacement of approximately 38,000 conventional streetlights citywide with energy-efficient LED fixtures.

- Centralized Control and Monitoring System (CCMS):
  • Installation of controllers and communication modules enabling remote switching, dimming, fault detection, and energy consumption monitoring.

- Energy Performance Contracts:
  • Implementation through an ESCO/PPP model where feasible, leveraging energy savings to finance upfront investments.

3. Integrated Command and Control Centre (ICCC) and City Data Platform:
- ICCC Facility:
  • A central control room with video walls, operator consoles, and communication systems for real-time monitoring of multiple services.

- System Integration:
  • Integration of data and controls from: IUMMS (bus GPS, PIS), smart street lighting CCMS, water supply SCADA (phased), CCTV surveillance, environmental sensors, and grievance management systems.

- City Data Platform and Dashboards:
  • A unified data repository and analytics layer supporting sector-specific and integrated dashboards for decision-makers.
  • Role-based access for different departments and public-facing dashboards for key indicators.

- Disaster and Emergency Management:
  • Coordination with police, fire, health, and disaster management agencies for incident response and coordination.

4. Unified Citizen Services Platform and Mobile App:
- Citizen Portal and Mobile App:
  • A single platform for property tax payments, water bill payments, solid waste service requests, building permission status, trade licenses, and other services.

- Grievance Redressal Integration:
  • Unified complaint registration and tracking system linked to departmental workflows and the ICCC, with SMS and app-based notifications.

- Multilingual and Inclusive Design:
  • Interfaces in English, Marathi, and Hindi, with simple user flows and support for assisted access at ward-level facilitation centres.

- Citizen Engagement Tools:
  • Online surveys, participatory budgeting modules, and feedback mechanisms on city projects and policies.

Together, these components form a cohesive pan-city smart infrastructure that enhances operational efficiency, user experience, transparency, and responsiveness of Nandigram’s urban governance.
part_c_pan_city_proposals.part_c_pan_city_proposals_21_approach_me:
The approach and methodology for Nandigram’s pan-city proposals emphasize interoperability, scalability, and integration with existing and planned systems.

1. Needs assessment and stakeholder engagement:
- Conducted workshops with NMC departments, NUTC, NUWSB, police, and disaster management authorities to identify pain points and priorities for ICT interventions.
- Engaged bus commuters, drivers, and transport unions to understand operational challenges and user expectations for the IUMMS.
- Collected feedback from citizens on desired digital services and usability considerations for the unified portal and app.

2. Architectural and technical design:
- Adopted a modular, service-oriented architecture for the ICCC and city data platform, allowing for phased integration of different systems.
- Ensured adherence to open standards and interoperability protocols to avoid vendor lock-in and facilitate future upgrades.
- Designed robust communication networks (fibre backbone, wireless links) with redundancy for critical systems.

3. Phased implementation strategy:
- Phase 1 (Years 1–2):
  • Establish basic ICCC infrastructure and integrate initial systems (CCTV feeds, grievance portal, LED streetlight CCMS).
  • Pilot GPS tracking and PIS on a subset of bus routes.
  • Launch basic version of the unified citizen portal and mobile app with core services (tax and bill payments, complaints).

- Phase 2 (Years 3–4):
  • Scale up IUMMS citywide (all buses, major stops, and terminals) and integrate adaptive traffic signal control at key junctions.
  • Expand ICCC integrations to include water supply SCADA (in phases), environmental sensors, and additional CCTV locations.
  • Add more services and features to the citizen platform (building permits, trade licenses, participatory tools).

- Phase 3 (Years 5+):
  • Refine systems based on performance data and user feedback.
  • Explore advanced analytics (predictive maintenance, demand forecasting) and open data initiatives.

4. Procurement and vendor management:
- Use of transparent, competitive bidding processes to select system integrators and service providers with proven experience.
- Development of detailed technical specifications and service level agreements (SLAs) to ensure reliability, uptime, and performance.
- Periodic performance reviews and penalties/incentives tied to SLA compliance.

5. Capacity building and change management:
- Training programs for municipal staff, bus operators, and other stakeholders on new systems and workflows.
- Change management initiatives to address resistance, clarify roles, and embed data-driven decision-making in daily operations.

6. Data governance, privacy, and security:
- Formulation of data governance policies addressing data ownership, sharing, retention, and quality.
- Implementation of cybersecurity measures (firewalls, encryption, access controls) and regular security audits.
- Compliance with applicable national and state regulations on data protection and privacy.

7. Monitoring, evaluation, and user feedback:
- Definition of KPIs (e.g., bus punctuality, average response time to complaints, energy savings, system uptime) and integration into ICCC dashboards.
- Regular user satisfaction surveys and usability testing for the citizen portal and app.
- Mechanisms for iterative improvements based on operational data and citizen feedback.

This methodology ensures that the pan-city initiatives are not just technology deployments but integrated governance reforms that enhance the everyday experience of living and working in Nandigram.
